title: Best Time to Buy & Sell Stock with Cooldown.
description: Best Time to Buy & Sell Stock with Cooldown.

examples.explanation: Start at day 0, not holding, no cooldown. Options: buy (profit -1, move to day 1 holding) or cooldown (profit 0, move to day 1 not holding). Explore buy path: day 1 holding, options: sell (profit -1+2=1, move to day 2 not holding with cooldown) or cooldown (profit -1, move to day 2 holding). Explore sell path: day 2 not holding with cooldown, must cooldown (profit 1, move to day 3 not holding no cooldown). Day 3 not holding, options: buy (profit 1-0=1, move to day 4 holding) or cooldown (profit 1, move to day 4 not holding). Explore buy: day 4 holding, must sell (profit 1+2=3). Backtrack, other paths yield lower profits. Max profit = 3.
Only one day, start not holding. Options: buy (profit -1, then no days left to sell, invalid) or cooldown (profit 0). Max profit = 0.

approaches.name: Brute Force Recursion O(2^n)
approaches.summary: Explore all possible sequences of buy/sell/cooldown decisions using recursion with memoization. This approach systematically checks every combination of actions at each day.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Brute Force Recursion with Memoization for Stock Trading with Cooldown
ladders.desc: Complete recursive solution exploring all buy/sell/cooldown sequences with memoization to maximize profit.
ladders.explanation: This approach solves the complete problem by exploring all possible sequences of actions (buy, sell, cooldown) at each day using recursion with memoization. The intuition is that at any given day, we can be in one of two states: holding a stock (bought previously) or not holding a stock (sold or never bought). When not holding, we can either buy (if we haven't bought recently) or cooldown (do nothing). When holding, we can either sell or cooldown. A cooldown day is required after selling before buying again. The recursion explores all combinations: for each day, based on current state (holding or not) and cooldown status, we try all valid actions, compute profits, and choose the maximum. Memoization stores results for (day, holding, cooldown) tuples to avoid redundant calculations. The base case is at the last day: if holding, we must sell to realize profit; if not holding, we do nothing. The algorithm starts at day 0, not holding, no cooldown. Time complexity is O(2^n) without memoization due to exponential decision tree, but with memoization it's O(n) since there are n days × 2 holding states × 2 cooldown states = O(4n) = O(n) subproblems. Space complexity is O(n) for recursion stack and memoization table. This feeds into the parent problem by providing a complete, albeit inefficient, solution that can be optimized to dynamic programming. Common mistakes include forgetting the cooldown constraint after selling, not handling base cases correctly, and missing memoization for all state variables. Real code pattern uses a recursive function with parameters: day index, holding boolean, cooldown boolean, returning max profit from that state.

examples.trace: Start at day 0, not holding, no cooldown. Options: buy (profit -1, move to day 1 holding) or cooldown (profit 0, move to day 1 not holding). Explore buy path: day 1 holding, options: sell (profit -1+2=1, move to day 2 not holding with cooldown) or cooldown (profit -1, move to day 2 holding). Explore sell path: day 2 not holding with cooldown, must cooldown (profit 1, move to day 3 not holding no cooldown). Day 3 not holding, options: buy (profit 1-0=1, move to day 4 holding) or cooldown (profit 1, move to day 4 not holding). Explore buy: day 4 holding, must sell (profit 1+2=3). Backtrack, other paths yield lower profits. Max profit = 3.
Only one day, start not holding. Options: buy (profit -1, then no days left to sell, invalid) or cooldown (profit 0). Max profit = 0.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Recursive Decision Function with State Tracking
ladders.desc: Recursive function that determines maximum profit from a given day with current holding and cooldown states.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the core recursive function that computes the maximum profit achievable starting from a specific day, given whether we're currently holding a stock and whether we're in a cooldown period (just sold). The intuition is that profit maximization can be broken down into subproblems: at each day, based on our state, we make optimal decisions for the remaining days. The function takes parameters: day index i, holding flag (true if holding a stock, false otherwise), cooldown flag (true if we sold on previous day and must cool down, false otherwise). It returns the maximum profit from day i to the end. The algorithm: if i == n (end of prices), return 0 (no more days to trade). If holding is true, options are: sell at day i (profit = prices[i] + recursive call at day i+1 with holding=false, cooldown=true) or cooldown (do nothing, profit = recursive call at day i+1 with holding=true, cooldown=false). Choose max. If holding is false, options depend on cooldown: if cooldown is true, must cooldown (profit = recursive call at day i+1 with holding=false, cooldown=false). If cooldown is false, options are: buy at day i (profit = -prices[i] + recursive call at day i+1 with holding=true, cooldown=false) or cooldown (profit = recursive call at day i+1 with holding=false, cooldown=false). Choose max. Time complexity per call is O(1) but without memoization leads to O(2^n) overall due to repeated calls. Space complexity is O(n) for recursion depth. This feeds into L0 by being the main recursive engine; L0 adds memoization and initial call. Common mistakes: incorrect state transitions, especially after sell; not handling cooldown flag properly; missing base case. Real code pattern: def dfs(i, holding, cooldown): if i == len(prices): return 0; if holding: sell = prices[i] + dfs(i+1, False, True); cooldown_hold = dfs(i+1, True, False); return max(sell, cooldown_hold); else: if cooldown: return dfs(i+1, False, False); buy = -prices[i] + dfs(i+1, True, False); cooldown_not_hold = dfs(i+1, False, False); return max(buy, cooldown_not_hold).

examples.trace: Day 0, not holding, no cooldown. Options: buy (profit -1 + dfs(1, True, False)) or cooldown (profit 0 + dfs(1, False, False)). Compute dfs(1, True, False): holding at day 1, options: sell (profit 2 + dfs(2, False, True)) or cooldown (profit 0 + dfs(2, True, False)). dfs(2,...) base returns 0. So sell = 2, cooldown = 0, max=2. Thus buy path profit = -1+2=1. dfs(1, False, False): not holding, no cooldown, options: buy (profit -2 + dfs(2, True, False)= -2+0=-2) or cooldown (0). Max=0. Thus cooldown path profit = 0+0=0. Overall max=1.
Day 0, holding, no cooldown. Options: sell (profit 3 + dfs(1, False, True)) or cooldown (profit 0 + dfs(1, True, False)). dfs(1,...) base returns 0 for both. So sell=3, cooldown=0, max=3.

ladders.title: Core Logic — State Transitions for Buy/Sell/Cooldown Decisions
ladders.desc: Defining the rules for transitioning between holding/not-holding and cooldown states based on actions.
ladders.explanation: This core logic defines how our state (holding and cooldown flags) changes when we take actions (buy, sell, cooldown) at each day. The intuition is that the trading problem can be modeled as a state machine where each day we decide an action, which updates our state and affects future decisions. There are two main state variables: holding (boolean: true if we own a stock, false otherwise) and cooldown (boolean: true if we sold on the previous day and cannot buy today, false otherwise). Actions: 1) Buy: allowed only if holding is false and cooldown is false. Effect: holding becomes true, cooldown becomes false (since buy doesn't trigger cooldown). Profit change: -price[i]. 2) Sell: allowed only if holding is true. Effect: holding becomes false, cooldown becomes true (since after selling, next day must be cooldown). Profit change: +price[i]. 3) Cooldown (do nothing): always allowed. Effect: holding remains same, cooldown becomes false (if it was true, it expires after one day; if false, stays false). Profit change: 0. The key insight is that cooldown only lasts one day and is only set after a sell. This logic feeds into L1 by providing the transition rules used in the recursive function; L1 implements these transitions in code. Time and space analysis: each transition is O(1), but the number of possible sequences grows exponentially. Common mistakes: allowing buy during cooldown, not resetting cooldown after one day, incorrect profit calculations. Real code pattern: transitions as conditional checks in recursion, e.g., if not holding and not cooldown: can buy; if holding: can sell; always can cooldown.

examples.trace: Initial state: not holding, no cooldown. Buy allowed because holding false and cooldown false. After buy: holding becomes true (now own stock), cooldown remains false (buy doesn't cause cooldown). Profit decreases by price 10.

Initial state: holding stock, no cooldown. Sell allowed because holding true. After sell: holding becomes false (no stock), cooldown becomes true (must cool down next day). Profit increases by price 15.

ladders.title: Building Block — Recursive Exploration of Decision Trees
ladders.desc: Using recursion to explore all possible sequences of actions over multiple days.
ladders.explanation: This building block involves using recursion to systematically explore all possible sequences of actions (buy, sell, cooldown) across days, building a decision tree. The intuition is that at each day, we have a set of valid actions based on current state; each action leads to a new state for the next day, forming branches in a tree. Recursion naturally models this by making a recursive call for each action, exploring depth-first. The algorithm: define a recursive function that takes current day index and state; if at end of days, return 0 profit; otherwise, for each valid action, compute profit for that action plus recursive call for next day with updated state; return maximum profit among actions. This is a brute-force approach without optimization. Time complexity is O(3^n) in worst case since each day could have up to 3 actions, but constraints reduce it; with cooldown, it's roughly O(2^n). Space complexity is O(n) for recursion depth. This feeds into L2 by providing the mechanism to apply state transitions repeatedly; L2 defines the transitions, L3 implements the exploration. Common mistakes: infinite recursion if base case missing, not exploring all actions, incorrect profit accumulation. Real code pattern: def explore(day, state): if day >= n: return 0; max_profit = -inf; for action in valid_actions(state): new_state, profit_change = apply_action(state, action, prices[day]); max_profit = max(max_profit, profit_change + explore(day+1, new_state)); return max_profit.

examples.trace: Day 0: state not holding, no cooldown. Valid actions: buy, cooldown. Branch 1 (buy): profit -1, new state holding=True, cooldown=False. Day 1: state holding, valid actions: sell, cooldown. Branch 1.1 (sell): profit +2, total profit -1+2=1, new state holding=False, cooldown=True, end day 2 base profit 0. Branch 1.2 (cooldown): profit 0, total profit -1+0=-1, new state holding=True, cooldown=False, end day 2 base profit 0. Branch 2 (cooldown): profit 0, new state holding=False, cooldown=False. Day 1: state not holding, no cooldown, valid actions: buy, cooldown. Branch 2.1 (buy): profit -2, total profit 0-2=-2, new state holding=True, cooldown=False, end day 2 base profit 0. Branch 2.2 (cooldown): profit 0, total profit 0+0=0. Max profit among leaves: 1.

Day 0: state holding, valid actions: sell, cooldown. Branch 1 (sell): profit +3, total profit 3, new state holding=False, cooldown=True, end day 1 base profit 0. Branch 2 (cooldown): profit 0, total profit 0, new state holding=True, cooldown=False, end day 1 base profit 0. Max profit = 3.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Action Validation and State Update
ladders.desc: Checking if an action is allowed given current state and updating state and profit accordingly.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves two steps: first, validating whether a specific action (buy, sell, or cooldown) is permitted based on the current holding and cooldown states; second, if allowed, computing the new state and profit change. The intuition is that each action has preconditions and effects that must be applied correctly to model the trading rules. Validation rules: buy requires holding=false and cooldown=false; sell requires holding=true; cooldown is always allowed. Update rules: for buy, set holding=true, cooldown=false, profit change = -price; for sell, set holding=false, cooldown=true, profit change = +price; for cooldown, holding unchanged, set cooldown=false (cooldown expires after one day), profit change = 0. This operation is O(1) in time and space. It feeds into L3 by being the atomic step in recursive exploration; L3 uses this repeatedly for each action at each day. Common mistakes: forgetting to reset cooldown to false after cooldown action, incorrect profit signs, allowing invalid actions. Real code pattern: function apply_action(state, action, price): if action == 'buy': if not state.holding and not state.cooldown: return new_state(holding=True, cooldown=False), -price; else: return invalid. Similar for sell and cooldown.

examples.output: valid, new state: holding=True, cooldown=False, profit_delta=-10
examples.trace: Check buy conditions: holding is false, cooldown is false, so valid. Update: holding becomes true, cooldown becomes false (buy doesn't trigger cooldown). Profit decreases by price 10.
Check buy conditions: holding is false (ok), but cooldown is true (not ok), so buy not allowed. Return invalid.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding State Variables in Decision Problems
ladders.desc: Using boolean flags to represent conditions that affect future decisions.
ladders.explanation: This foundation concept introduces the idea of using state variables, specifically boolean flags, to track conditions that influence what actions can be taken in a decision-making process. The intuition is that many problems involve sequences of decisions where past actions affect future options; state variables encapsulate this history concisely. Here, we use two boolean flags: 'holding' indicates if we currently own a stock (true) or not (false); 'cooldown' indicates if we sold a stock on the previous day and must wait one day before buying again (true) or not (false). These flags are necessary because: without 'holding', we wouldn't know if we can sell; without 'cooldown', we might buy immediately after selling, violating the cooldown rule. State variables reduce infinite history to finite memory. They are updated after each action. This concept feeds into L4 by providing the state representation that L4 validates and updates. Time and space: each flag is O(1), but combinations (2×2=4 states) define the state space. Common mistakes: using too many or too few state variables, not updating them correctly. Real code pattern: represent state as a tuple or object with booleans, e.g., (holding, cooldown).

examples.input: Initial state: holding=False, cooldown=False. Action: buy stock.
examples.trace: Start with no stock and no cooldown. After buying, we now hold a stock, so holding becomes true. Buying does not trigger cooldown, so cooldown remains false.

examples.input: State: holding=True, cooldown=False. Action: sell stock.
examples.trace: Start holding a stock, no cooldown. After selling, we no longer hold stock, so holding becomes false. Selling triggers a cooldown period, so cooldown becomes true for the next day.

approaches.name: DP with Three States O(n)
approaches.summary: Maintain three DP arrays representing states: holding stock, sold today (in cooldown), and not holding but not in cooldown. Transition between states based on buy/sell rules.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Best Time to Buy & Sell Stock with Cooldown using DP with Three States
ladders.desc: Complete solution using DP arrays for three states: holding, sold today (cooldown), and not holding but not in cooldown.
ladders.explanation: This problem requires maximizing profit from stock transactions with a cooldown period after selling. The DP with three states approach models the system's possible states at each day. We maintain three DP arrays: hold[i] = max profit ending day i holding a stock, sold[i] = max profit ending day i having just sold (entering cooldown), and rest[i] = max profit ending day i not holding and not in cooldown. The intuition is that these three states capture all possible scenarios: you can either hold a stock (bought earlier), have just sold (so next day must be cooldown), or be resting (not holding and not in cooldown, so can buy). Transitions: hold[i] = max(hold[i-1], rest[i-1] - prices[i]) (either keep holding or buy today from rest state), sold[i] = hold[i-1] + prices[i] (sell today from hold state), rest[i] = max(rest[i-1], sold[i-1]) (either stay resting or come from cooldown). Initialize hold[0] = -prices[0] (buy on day 0), sold[0] = -inf (cannot sell on day 0), rest[0] = 0. The answer is max(sold[n-1], rest[n-1]) since holding at end gives no profit. Time O(n), space O(n). Connection: This integrates all lower-level concepts into the full solution. Common mistakes: forgetting to initialize sold[0] properly, incorrect transition logic, or not handling negative infinity for impossible states. Real code pattern: def maxProfit(prices): n = len(prices); if n < 2: return 0; hold, sold, rest = [-prices[0]] + [0]*(n-1), [float('-inf')] + [0]*(n-1), [0] + [0]*(n-1); for i in range(1, n): hold[i] = max(hold[i-1], rest[i-1] - prices[i]); sold[i] = hold[i-1] + prices[i]; rest[i] = max(rest[i-1], sold[i-1]); return max(sold[-1], rest[-1]).

examples.trace: prices = [1,2,3,0,2], n=5. Initialize: hold[0]=-1, sold[0]=-inf, rest[0]=0. Day 1 (i=1, price=2): hold[1]=max(hold[0]=-1, rest[0]-2=0-2=-2) = -1; sold[1]=hold[0]+2=-1+2=1; rest[1]=max(rest[0]=0, sold[0]=-inf) = 0. Day 2 (i=2, price=3): hold[2]=max(hold[1]=-1, rest[1]-3=0-3=-3) = -1; sold[2]=hold[1]+3=-1+3=2; rest[2]=max(rest[1]=0, sold[1]=1) = 1. Day 3 (i=3, price=0): hold[3]=max(hold[2]=-1, rest[2]-0=1-0=1) = 1; sold[3]=hold[2]+0=-1+0=-1; rest[3]=max(rest[2]=1, sold[2]=2) = 2. Day 4 (i=4, price=2): hold[4]=max(hold[3]=1, rest[3]-2=2-2=0) = 1; sold[4]=hold[3]+2=1+2=3; rest[4]=max(rest[3]=2, sold[3]=-1) = 2. Final: max(sold[4]=3, rest[4]=2) = 3.
prices = [1], n=1. Initialize: hold[0]=-1, sold[0]=-inf, rest[0]=0. No iterations. Final: max(sold[0]=-inf, rest[0]=0) = 0.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — DP State Transitions for Stock with Cooldown
ladders.desc: Implement the transition logic between hold, sold, and rest states for each day.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine focuses on updating the three DP arrays (hold, sold, rest) from day i-1 to day i based on the stock price. The intuition is that at each day, you can be in one of three states, and transitions depend on allowed actions: from hold, you can stay hold or sell; from sold, you must go to rest (cooldown); from rest, you can stay rest or buy (go to hold). The algorithm: given arrays hold, sold, rest of length n, and prices array, for i from 1 to n-1: hold[i] = max(hold[i-1], rest[i-1] - prices[i]) (either keep holding from previous day, or buy today from rest state, costing price), sold[i] = hold[i-1] + prices[i] (sell today from hold state, adding price to profit), rest[i] = max(rest[i-1], sold[i-1]) (either stay resting, or come from sold state after cooldown). Time O(n) for the loop, space O(1) per iteration if reusing variables. Connection: This is the core update step in the full problem. Common mistakes: using wrong indices, incorrect arithmetic (e.g., adding instead of subtracting for buy), or not considering all transitions. Real code pattern: for i in range(1, n): new_hold = max(hold, rest - prices[i]); new_sold = hold + prices[i]; new_rest = max(rest, sold); hold, sold, rest = new_hold, new_sold, new_rest.

examples.trace: Given previous day: hold=-1, sold=-inf, rest=0. Current price=2. Compute: new_hold = max(hold=-1, rest - price=0-2=-2) = -1; new_sold = hold + price = -1+2=1; new_rest = max(rest=0, sold=-inf) = 0. Output: hold=-1, sold=1, rest=0.

Given previous day: hold=1, sold=2, rest=1. Current price=0. Compute: new_hold = max(hold=1, rest - price=1-0=1) = 1; new_sold = hold + price = 1+0=1? Wait, correct: hold=1, price=0, so 1+0=1, but sold should be from previous hold? In trace, sold uses previous hold: sold = hold_prev + price = 1+0=1, but in example output sold=-1? Let's recalc: Actually, in this sub-routine, input is previous state, so hold=1 is previous hold. Then new_sold = hold + price = 1+0=1. But output says sold=-1, which seems inconsistent. Let me correct: For consistency, use example from full problem: Day 3 in first example: previous hold= -1, sold=2, rest=1, price=0. Then new_hold = max(-1, 1-0=1) =1, new_sold = -1+0=-1, new_rest = max(1,2)=2. So input: hold=-1, sold=2, rest=1, price=0; output: hold=1, sold=-1, rest=2.

ladders.title: Core Logic — State Machine for Stock Transactions with Cooldown
ladders.desc: Model the problem as a state machine with three states and transitions based on buy/sell/cooldown rules.
ladders.explanation: The core insight is to represent each day's situation as one of three states: HOLD (owning a stock), SOLD (just sold, so next day is cooldown), and REST (not owning and not in cooldown). This forms a state machine where transitions are dictated by the problem constraints: from HOLD, you can stay HOLD (do nothing) or go to SOLD (sell); from SOLD, you must go to REST (cooldown); from REST, you can stay REST (do nothing) or go to HOLD (buy). The intuition is that this abstraction simplifies tracking profit and cooldown without complex conditionals. The algorithm: define states H, S, R. For each day with price p, update: H = max(previous H, previous R - p) (buy from REST costs p), S = previous H + p (sell from HOLD gains p), R = max(previous R, previous S) (cooldown from SOLD or stay). Time O(1) per transition, space O(1) for state variables. Connection: This state machine is implemented in the DP arrays. Common mistakes: adding extra states, incorrect transition directions, or misinterpreting cooldown (e.g., allowing buy immediately after sell). Real code pattern: H, S, R = -prices[0], float('-inf'), 0; for p in prices[1:]: new_H = max(H, R - p); new_S = H + p; new_R = max(R, S); H, S, R = new_H, new_S, new_R.

examples.trace: Start: H=-1, S=-inf, R=0. Day 1 price=2: new_H = max(H=-1, R-p=0-2=-2) = -1; new_S = H+p=-1+2=1; new_R = max(R=0, S=-inf)=0; update: H=-1, S=1, R=0. Day 2 price=3: new_H = max(-1, 0-3=-3)=-1; new_S = -1+3=2; new_R = max(0,1)=1; update: H=-1, S=2, R=1. Day 3 price=0: new_H = max(-1, 1-0=1)=1; new_S = -1+0=-1; new_R = max(1,2)=2; update: H=1, S=-1, R=2. Day 4 price=2: new_H = max(1, 2-2=0)=1; new_S = 1+2=3; new_R = max(2,-1)=2; update: H=1, S=3, R=2. Final: H=1, S=3, R=2.

Start: H=-7, S=-inf, R=0. Day 1 price=1: new_H = max(-7, 0-1=-1)=-1; new_S = -7+1=-6; new_R = max(0,-inf)=0; update: H=-1, S=-6, R=0. Day 2 price=5: new_H = max(-1,0-5=-5)=-1; new_S = -1+5=4; new_R = max(0,-6)=0; update: H=-1, S=4, R=0. Day 3 price=3: new_H = max(-1,0-3=-3)=-1; new_S = -1+3=2; new_R = max(0,4)=4; update: H=-1, S=2, R=4. Day 4 price=6: new_H = max(-1,4-6=-2)=-1; new_S = -1+6=5; new_R = max(4,2)=4; update: H=-1, S=5, R=4. Day 5 price=4: new_H = max(-1,4-4=0)=0; new_S = -1+4=3; new_R = max(4,5)=5; update: H=0, S=3, R=5. Final: H=0, S=3, R=5? Wait, output says S=5, R=5, but in trace S=3. Let's recalc: At day 4, H=-1, S=5, R=4. Day 5 price=4: new_H = max(H=-1, R-p=4-4=0) = 0; new_S = H+p = -1+4=3; new_R = max(R=4, S=5)=5. So final H=0, S=3, R=5. But output expects S=5, R=5. Perhaps error in example. Let's use correct from full problem: For prices [7,1,5,3,6,4], max profit=5. Start H=-7, S=-inf, R=0. Day1 p=1: H=max(-7,0-1=-1)=-1, S=-7+1=-6, R=max(0,-inf)=0. Day2 p=5: H=max(-1,0-5=-5)=-1, S=-1+5=4, R=max(0,-6)=0. Day3 p=3: H=max(-1,0-3=-3)=-1, S=-1+3=2, R=max(0,4)=4. Day4 p=6: H=max(-1,4-6=-2)=-1, S=-1+6=5, R=max(4,2)=4. Day5 p=4: H=max(-1,4-4=0)=0, S=-1+4=3, R=max(4,5)=5. Final H=0, S=3, R=5, max(S,R)=5. So example output should be H=0, S=3, R=5. Correcting: input: Initial H=-7, S=-inf, R=0, prices=[1,5,3,6,4]; output: Final H=0, S=3, R=5.

ladders.title: Building Block — Dynamic Programming with Multiple States
ladders.desc: Use DP to track multiple states (e.g., hold, sold, rest) and update them iteratively.
ladders.explanation: This building block introduces DP where you maintain several state variables that represent different conditions, updating them based on transitions. The intuition is that for problems with multiple possible states per step, DP can efficiently compute optimal values by considering all state changes. The algorithm: initialize state variables (e.g., hold, sold, rest) for day 0. For each subsequent day, compute new values using previous states and current input (e.g., price), then update. This avoids exponential recursion by storing only previous states. Time O(n) for n days, space O(1) if reusing variables. Connection: This general DP technique is applied to the specific states in the stock problem. Common mistakes: not initializing properly, updating states in wrong order (should compute all new values before updating), or using arrays unnecessarily. Real code pattern: state1, state2, state3 = init1, init2, init3; for value in values[1:]: new_state1 = f(state1, state2, state3, value); new_state2 = g(state1, state2, state3, value); new_state3 = h(state1, state2, state3, value); state1, state2, state3 = new_state1, new_state2, new_state3.

examples.input: Initial states: A=0, B=1, C=2; update rules: new_A = max(A, B); new_B = A + C; new_C = B - C; values=[3,4]
examples.trace: Start: A=0, B=1, C=2. First value=3: new_A = max(A=0, B=1) = 1; new_B = A + value = 0+3=3; new_C = B - value = 1-3=-2; update: A=1, B=3, C=-2. Second value=4: new_A = max(1,3)=3; new_B = A+value=1+4=5; new_C = B-value=3-4=-1; update: A=3, B=5, C=-1. Final: A=3, B=5, C=-1? But output says C=-2. Let's recalc: After first update, C=-2. Second value=4: new_C = B - value = 3-4=-1, so C becomes -1. Output should be A=3, B=5, C=-1. Correcting example: input: Initial A=0, B=1, C=2; rules: new_A=max(A,B), new_B=A+val, new_C=B-val; values=[3,4]; output: Final A=3, B=5, C=-1.

examples.input: Initial states: X=5, Y=3, Z=0; update rules: new_X = X + Y; new_Y = max(Y, Z); new_Z = X - Y; values=[2,1]
examples.trace: Start: X=5, Y=3, Z=0. First value=2: new_X = X+Y=5+3=8; new_Y = max(Y=3, Z=0)=3; new_Z = X-Y=5-3=2; update: X=8, Y=3, Z=2. Second value=1: new_X = X+Y=8+3=11; new_Y = max(3,2)=3; new_Z = X-Y=8-3=5; update: X=11, Y=3, Z=5. Final: X=11, Y=3, Z=5, but output says X=13, Y=5, Z=2. Error in example. Let's design simple correct example: For stock-like: hold=-1, sold=-inf, rest=0, price=2: new_hold=max(hold, rest-price)=max(-1,0-2=-2)=-1; new_sold=hold+price=-1+2=1; new_rest=max(rest, sold)=max(0,-inf)=0. So input: hold=-1, sold=-inf, rest=0, price=2; output: hold=-1, sold=1, rest=0.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Updating Maximum Profit with Buy/Sell Actions
ladders.desc: Compute new profit values when buying or selling a stock at a given price.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves calculating profit changes from buying or selling a stock. The intuition is that buying reduces profit by the price (cost), and selling increases profit by the price (revenue). For DP, we update profit values based on current state: e.g., if holding, profit is previous profit minus buy price; if selling, profit is previous profit plus sell price. The algorithm: given previous profit values for states hold, sold, rest, and current price p, compute new_hold = max(previous_hold, previous_rest - p) (buying from rest state), new_sold = previous_hold + p (selling from hold state), new_rest = max(previous_rest, previous_sold) (no transaction). Time O(1), space O(1). Connection: This operation is used in each DP transition. Common mistakes: incorrect sign for buy (should subtract), using wrong previous state, or not taking max appropriately. Real code pattern: new_hold = max(old_hold, old_rest - price); new_sold = old_hold + price; new_rest = max(old_rest, old_sold).

examples.trace: To compute new_hold: option1 = old_hold = -1 (keep holding), option2 = old_rest - price = 0 - 2 = -2 (buy from rest). Take max: max(-1, -2) = -1. So new_hold = -1.
To compute new_sold: new_sold = old_hold + price = -1 + 3 = 2. This represents selling the stock held from previous day.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Profit from Stock Transactions
ladders.desc: Calculate profit from buying low and selling high in a sequence of stock prices.
ladders.explanation: This foundational concept involves understanding how profit is generated in stock trading: you buy a stock at some price and later sell it at a higher price, with profit being the difference. For a sequence of prices, you aim to maximize total profit by choosing when to buy and sell. The intuition is simple: profit = sell price - buy price. If you have multiple transactions, total profit is sum of individual profits. In problems, we often track maximum profit achievable up to each day. The algorithm: initialize max_profit = 0, min_price = first price. For each price in the list: if price < min_price, update min_price; else, compute profit = price - min_price and update max_profit = max(max_profit, profit). Time O(n), space O(1). Connection: This basic idea extends to more complex scenarios with cooldown. Common mistakes: not initializing min_price properly, or updating min_price after computing profit. Real code pattern: max_profit = 0; min_price = prices[0]; for p in prices[1:]: if p < min_price: min_price = p; else: max_profit = max(max_profit, p - min_price).

examples.trace: prices = [7,1,5,3,6,4]. Initialize: max_profit=0, min_price=7. Day1 price=1: 1 < 7, so min_price=1. Day2 price=5: 5 > 1, profit=5-1=4, max_profit=max(0,4)=4. Day3 price=3: 3 > 1, profit=3-1=2, max_profit=max(4,2)=4. Day4 price=6: 6 > 1, profit=6-1=5, max_profit=max(4,5)=5. Day5 price=4: 4 > 1, profit=4-1=3, max_profit=max(5,3)=5. Final max_profit=5.

prices = [1,2,3,4,5]. Initialize: max_profit=0, min_price=1. Day1 price=2: 2 > 1, profit=2-1=1, max_profit=1. Day2 price=3: 3 > 1, profit=3-1=2, max_profit=2. Day3 price=4: 4 > 1, profit=4-1=3, max_profit=3. Day4 price=5: 5 > 1, profit=5-1=4, max_profit=4. Final max_profit=4.

approaches.name: DP with Two States O(n)
approaches.summary: Use only two states: holding and not holding, while tracking the maximum profit from two days ago to handle cooldown. This reduces state complexity while maintaining correctness.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Best Time to Buy & Sell Stock with Cooldown using Two-State DP
ladders.desc: Complete solution using DP with only two states (holding, not holding) while tracking profit from two days ago to handle cooldown.
ladders.explanation: This problem requires finding maximum profit from stock transactions with a cooldown period: after selling, you must wait one day before buying again. The two-state DP approach elegantly handles this constraint with O(n) time and O(1) space. We maintain two variables: 'hold' (maximum profit when holding a stock at day i) and 'free' (maximum profit when not holding a stock at day i). The key insight is that when transitioning from selling to buying, we need to skip one day due to cooldown. We achieve this by tracking the 'free' value from two days ago (prevFree). At each day i: hold[i] = max(hold[i-1], prevFree - prices[i]) (either keep holding or buy today using profit from two days ago), free[i] = max(free[i-1], hold[i-1] + prices[i]) (either stay free or sell today). We initialize hold[0] = -prices[0] (buy on day 0), free[0] = 0 (do nothing). The answer is free[n-1] since we want to end without holding stock. Time: O(n) iterating through prices once. Space: O(1) using only a few variables. Connection to L1: This integrates the state transition logic with cooldown handling. Common mistakes: Forgetting to update prevFree correctly, incorrectly initializing hold[0], or not understanding why we use prevFree instead of free[i-1] for buying. Real code pattern: Initialize hold = -prices[0], free = 0, prevFree = 0. For i from 1 to n-1: temp = free; hold = max(hold, prevFree - prices[i]); free = max(free, hold + prices[i]); prevFree = temp.

examples.trace:
Day 0: hold = -1, free = 0, prevFree = 0
Day 1: temp = free = 0; hold = max(hold=-1, prevFree=0 - prices[1]=2) = max(-1, -2) = -1; free = max(free=0, hold=-1 + prices[1]=2) = max(0, 1) = 1; prevFree = temp = 0
Day 2: temp = free = 1; hold = max(hold=-1, prevFree=0 - prices[2]=3) = max(-1, -3) = -1; free = max(free=1, hold=-1 + prices[2]=3) = max(1, 2) = 2; prevFree = temp = 1
Day 3: temp = free = 2; hold = max(hold=-1, prevFree=1 - prices[3]=0) = max(-1, 1) = 1; free = max(free=2, hold=1 + prices[3]=0) = max(2, 1) = 2; prevFree = temp = 2
Day 4: temp = free = 2; hold = max(hold=1, prevFree=2 - prices[4]=2) = max(1, 0) = 1; free = max(free=2, hold=1 + prices[4]=2) = max(2, 3) = 3; prevFree = temp = 2
Final free = 3

Day 0: hold = -1, free = 0, prevFree = 0
Only one day, cannot sell. Final free = 0

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — State Transitions with Cooldown Constraint
ladders.desc: Implement the core DP transitions for hold and free states while properly handling the one-day cooldown after selling.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine focuses on the state transition logic that makes the two-state DP work. We have two states: hold (max profit when holding stock) and free (max profit when not holding stock). The challenge is enforcing the cooldown: after selling, we cannot buy the next day. The solution is to use 'prevFree' - the free value from two days ago. When buying on day i, we use prevFree (not free[i-1]) because free[i-1] might include profit from selling on day i-1, which would violate cooldown. Transitions: hold = max(keep holding from previous day, buy today using profit from two days ago), free = max(keep free from previous day, sell today). Algorithm: Initialize hold = -prices[0], free = 0, prevFree = 0. For i from 1 to n-1: temp = free; hold = max(hold, prevFree - prices[i]); free = max(free, hold + prices[i]); prevFree = temp. Time: O(n). Space: O(1). Connection to L0: This is the main loop of the full solution. Connection to L2: This implements the insight of using prevFree. Common mistakes: Using free[i-1] instead of prevFree for buying, not updating prevFree correctly, or confusing the order of updates. Real code pattern: Same as described.

examples.trace:
Day 1: temp = free = 0; hold = max(hold=-1, prevFree=0 - prices[1]=2) = max(-1, -2) = -1; free = max(free=0, hold=-1 + prices[1]=2) = max(0, 1) = 1; prevFree = temp = 0
Day 2: temp = free = 1; hold = max(hold=-1, prevFree=0 - prices[2]=3) = max(-1, -3) = -1; free = max(free=1, hold=-1 + prices[2]=3) = max(1, 2) = 2; prevFree = temp = 1
Final: hold=-1, free=2

Day 1: temp = free = 0; hold = max(hold=-3, prevFree=0 - prices[1]=2) = max(-3, -2) = -2; free = max(free=0, hold=-2 + prices[1]=2) = max(0, 0) = 0; prevFree = temp = 0
Day 2: temp = free = 0; hold = max(hold=-2, prevFree=0 - prices[2]=1) = max(-2, -1) = -1; free = max(free=0, hold=-1 + prices[2]=1) = max(0, 0) = 0; prevFree = temp = 0
Final: hold=-1, free=0

ladders.title: Core Logic — Using Profit from Two Days Ago to Handle Cooldown
ladders.desc: Understand why we need to track free profit from two days ago instead of yesterday when buying.
ladders.explanation: This is the central insight of the two-state DP approach. The cooldown constraint means: if we sell on day i, we cannot buy on day i+1. In DP terms, when computing hold[i] (decision to buy on day i), we cannot use free[i-1] if it includes selling on day i-1. Solution: Use free[i-2] (profit from two days ago) for buying on day i. Since free[i-2] represents profit from day i-2 or earlier, it guarantees no sale occurred on day i-1, satisfying cooldown. We implement this by tracking 'prevFree' as free from two days ago. At day i: hold[i] = max(hold[i-1], prevFree - prices[i]). This ensures buying only after cooldown. Why it works: Any profitable sequence ending with buying on day i must have last sold on day i-2 or earlier. Time: O(1) per day. Space: O(1). Connection to L1: This insight drives the state transitions. Connection to L3: Builds on understanding of state definitions. Common mistakes: Thinking free[i-1] always works, or not seeing why two-day gap is necessary. Real code pattern: prevFree variable updated each iteration.

examples.input: Sequence: buy day0, sell day1, want to buy day2
examples.output: Cannot buy day2, must use profit from day0
examples.trace:
Day0: buy at price 1, hold profit = -1
Day1: sell at price 2, free profit = 1
Day2: want to buy. If we use free[1]=1 (includes sale on day1), violates cooldown. Instead use free[0]=0 (profit from two days ago). hold[2] = max(hold[1], free[0]-price2). This prevents immediate buy after sell.

examples.input: Sequence: buy day0, sell day2, want to buy day3
examples.output: Can buy day3 using profit from day2
examples.trace:
Day0: buy, hold=-1
Day1: hold, no sale
Day2: sell, free=profit
Day3: want to buy. free[2] includes sale on day2, so cannot use. Use free[1] (profit from two days ago). Since day1 had no sale, free[1] is safe. hold[3] = max(hold[2], free[1]-price3).

testCases.input: buy day0 sell day1, check day2 buy source
buy day0 sell day2, check day3 buy source
no sale before day3, check day3 buy source
sale day0, check day2 buy source
sale day1, check day3 buy source
multiple sales, check day5 buy source
never sell, check day4 buy source
sell every other day, check day6 buy source
buy day1 sell day2, check day4 buy source
complex sequence, check day7 buy source

ladders.title: Building Block — DP States for Stock Trading Without Cooldown
ladders.desc: Define and compute two DP states (holding, not holding) for basic stock trading problem.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept establishes the foundation for the two-state DP approach. For stock trading without cooldown (like Best Time to Buy and Sell Stock II), we can use two states: hold[i] = max profit ending with holding a stock on day i, free[i] = max profit ending with no stock on day i. Transitions: hold[i] = max(hold[i-1], free[i-1] - prices[i]) (either keep holding or buy today), free[i] = max(free[i-1], hold[i-1] + prices[i]) (either stay free or sell today). Initialize hold[0] = -prices[0] (buy on day0), free[0] = 0 (do nothing). Answer is free[n-1]. Intuition: At each day, we choose best action based on previous states. Time: O(n). Space: O(n) for DP arrays, reducible to O(1). Connection to L2: This is the base without cooldown; adding prevFree modifies it for cooldown. Connection to L4: Uses basic max operations. Common mistakes: Incorrect initialization, misunderstanding state definitions, or not seeing why two states suffice. Real code pattern: hold = [-prices[0]] + [0]*(n-1); free = [0]*n; for i in range(1,n): hold[i] = max(hold[i-1], free[i-1]-prices[i]); free[i] = max(free[i-1], hold[i-1]+prices[i]).

examples.trace:
Day0: hold[0]=-1, free[0]=0
Day1: hold[1] = max(hold[0]=-1, free[0]=0 - prices[1]=2) = max(-1, -2) = -1; free[1] = max(free[0]=0, hold[0]=-1 + prices[1]=2) = max(0, 1) = 1
Day2: hold[2] = max(hold[1]=-1, free[1]=1 - prices[2]=3) = max(-1, -2) = -1; free[2] = max(free[1]=1, hold[1]=-1 + prices[2]=3) = max(1, 2) = 2

Day0: hold[0]=-3, free[0]=0
Day1: hold[1] = max(hold[0]=-3, free[0]=0 - prices[1]=2) = max(-3, -2) = -2; free[1] = max(free[0]=0, hold[0]=-3 + prices[1]=2) = max(0, -1) = 0
Day2: hold[2] = max(hold[1]=-2, free[1]=0 - prices[2]=1) = max(-2, -1) = -1; free[2] = max(free[1]=0, hold[1]=-2 + prices[2]=1) = max(0, -1) = 0

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Computing Maximum Profit with Daily Decisions
ladders.desc: Given previous day's hold and free profits, compute today's best hold and free profits using buy/sell decisions.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation is the building block of the DP approach. Each day, we have two numbers: yesterday's hold profit (profit if holding stock) and free profit (profit if not holding). We compute today's values by considering possible actions: For hold today: option1 = keep holding (yesterday's hold), option2 = buy today (yesterday's free minus today's price). Choose max. For free today: option1 = stay free (yesterday's free), option2 = sell today (yesterday's hold plus today's price). Choose max. Mathematically: hold_today = max(hold_yesterday, free_yesterday - price_today); free_today = max(free_yesterday, hold_yesterday + price_today). Intuition: We always pick the action that gives higher profit. Time: O(1). Space: O(1). Connection to L3: This operation is applied repeatedly in the DP loop. Connection to L5: Uses basic max and arithmetic. Common mistakes: Subtracting price for buy when should add, confusing hold/free roles, or not using max correctly. Real code pattern: new_hold = max(old_hold, old_free - price); new_free = max(old_free, old_hold + price).

examples.trace:
Compute new_hold: max(keep holding = -5, buy today = old_free=3 - price=4 = -1) = max(-5, -1) = -1
Compute new_free: max(stay free = 3, sell today = old_hold=-5 + price=4 = -1) = max(3, -1) = 3

Compute new_hold: max(keep holding = 2, buy today = old_free=1 - price=6 = -5) = max(2, -5) = 2
Compute new_free: max(stay free = 1, sell today = old_hold=2 + price=6 = 8) = max(1, 8) = 8

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Profit from Buying and Selling Stocks
ladders.desc: Calculate profit from simple buy-sell transactions and understand hold/free states.
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite concept. In stock trading, profit = sell price - buy price. We define two states: 'holding a stock' means you have bought and not sold yet; 'free' means you have no stock. When holding, your current profit is negative (you paid the buy price) until you sell. When free, your profit is the cumulative profit from completed transactions. Example: Buy at price 10: hold profit = -10, free profit = 0. If price rises to 15 and you sell: hold profit becomes irrelevant (transaction complete), free profit = 5. Intuition: Hold represents potential future profit, free represents realized profit. We track both to decide when to buy/sell. Time: O(1) per transaction. Space: O(1). Connection to L4: This basic understanding leads to the DP operations. Common mistakes: Confusing profit signs, not separating hold/free states, or thinking profit can be positive while holding. Real code pattern: hold = -buy_price; free = 0; after sell: free = free + (sell_price - buy_price).

examples.trace:
After buy: hold = -5, free = 0
After sell: free = free + (sell_price - buy_price) = 0 + (8-5) = 3; hold becomes irrelevant (set to 0 or ignore)

examples.input: Buy at 10, price drops to 7, do not sell
examples.trace:
After buy: hold = -10, free = 0
Price changes to 7, but no sale: hold remains -10 (unrealized loss), free remains 0
